tree._: Project log for the garden wall rebuild. A cumulative history — living block with sequential digits and temporal mapping. Decimal 2 means the root is pscale 2 (broadest summary). Pscale 1 nodes summarize phases. Pscale 0 nodes summarize work weeks. Pscale -1 holds individual daily entries. When entries 1-9 fill at any level, they compress into their parent's underscore summary. Digit 0 would hold the compression product if needed. X+ zooms to a longer timespan, X- to a shorter one, X~ shows adjacent periods in sequence.

1._: Phase one: demolition and foundation. Three weeks of clearing the old wall and preparing footings. Ground conditions were worse than expected — roots from the neighbour's ash tree had displaced the original foundation entirely.
Week one: demolition. Removed the leaning section and salvaged usable stone. Roughly 60% of the old limestone is sound enough to reuse.
1.1: Monday. Assessed the wall. Lean measured at 15 degrees from vertical over the middle eight-foot section. Mortar crumbles by hand — pure lime, no cement, probably original 1890s build.
1.2: Tuesday. Set up scaffolding and began removing coping stones. Numbered each one with chalk for relaying in the same order. Eighteen coping stones, three cracked beyond reuse.
1.3: Wednesday. Took down the leaning section course by course. Found a clay land drain running through the foundation — blocked solid with root mass. This explains the subsidence.
1.4: Thursday. Cleared rubble and sorted stone into three piles: reuse, hardcore, discard. Discovered a carved date stone reading 1887 — set aside for reinstatement.
1.5: Friday. Finished demolition to ground level. Exposed the old foundation — or rather, the absence of one. The original wall sat on compacted earth with a single course of flat stones.

2._: Week two: excavation and drainage. Dug new foundation trench and installed drainage to prevent the problem recurring. Hit clay at eighteen inches.
2.1: Monday. Marked out the foundation trench — eighteen inches wide, twelve inches deep. Began digging by hand. Topsoil gave way to heavy clay at about ten inches.
2.2: Tuesday. Continued excavation. Removed the old blocked land drain entirely. Rerouted a new 4-inch perforated pipe along the wall line, bedded in gravel, falling to the existing ditch.
2.3: Wednesday. Rain stopped work after an hour. Trench filled with water — confirmed the drainage problem. Pumped out and noted the water table is high on this side of the garden.
2.4: Thursday. Completed trench. Lined with geotextile membrane over the gravel and pipe. Backfilled to formation level with compacted MOT Type 1 aggregate.
2.5: Friday. Ordered concrete for the foundation pour. Calculated 0.8 cubic metres. Checked levels — the ground falls 4 inches over the wall's 30-foot length. Stepped foundation needed at the midpoint.

3._: Week three: foundation. Poured and cured the concrete foundation in two steps to follow the ground level. Lime mortar test batch mixed while waiting.
3.1: Monday. Built formwork for the stepped foundation. Lower step: 15 feet long. Upper step: 15 feet long, 4 inches higher. Checked levels three times.
3.2: Tuesday. Concrete delivered and poured. Tamped and levelled. Covered with plastic sheeting for curing. Temperature around 8 degrees — slow cure expected.
3.3: Wednesday. Foundation curing. Used the day to clean salvaged stone with a wire brush and sort by size for coursing.
3.4: Thursday. Foundation curing. Prepared lime mortar — 1:3 NHL 3.5 to sharp sand. Mixed a test batch and checked workability. Slightly too dry; adjusted water ratio.
3.5: Friday. Stripped formwork. Foundation solid and level. Ready to begin building on Monday. Laid out first course dry to check bond pattern and confirm stone quantities.

2._: Phase two: rebuilding. Four weeks of laying stone, rebuilding the wall to its original height with improved mortar and proper foundations. Lost one full week to hard frost.

1._: Week four: first courses. Laid the base courses in salvaged limestone, bedded in lime mortar. Established the bond pattern and built up corners first as tradition requires.
1.1: Monday. Laid the first course on both steps of the foundation. Largest stones at the base, as tradition demands. Mortar joints kept to 10mm.
1.2: Tuesday. Built up the corners to four courses — corners lead, infill follows. Checked plumb at every course.
1.3: Wednesday. Infilled between corners. Three courses by end of day. The salvaged stone fits well — irregular faces give good character.
1.4: Thursday. Continued coursing. Reached the height of the stepped foundation transition. Bonded the two levels together with through-stones spanning the joint.
1.5: Friday. Five courses complete. Wall height at 22 inches. Covered the top with hessian to protect the green mortar from weekend rain.

2._: Week five: mid-height. Reached half the final wall height. Embedded wall ties and reinstated the 1887 date stone at eye level.
2.1: Monday. Continued building. Inserted stainless steel wall ties at every fourth course — the neighbour may build against this wall in future.
2.2: Tuesday. Reached 36 inches — roughly half height. Checked plumb, level, and line. All within tolerance.
2.3: Wednesday. Reinstated the 1887 date stone at what will be eye level, bedded in mortar with a slight recess so it reads as a feature.
2.4: Thursday. Worked on the section where the old drain had caused subsidence. Extra care here — doubled the through-stones and kept joints tight.
2.5: Friday. Paused to let the lower courses cure under load before adding more weight. Spent the afternoon cutting stone for the coping course.

3._: Weeks six and seven: completion. Lost week six entirely to hard frost. Completed the wall in week seven, including coping stones and pointing.
3.1: Week six was a write-off. Temperatures below freezing all week. Lime mortar cannot be laid in frost — it will fail. Covered the wall and waited.
3.2: Monday of week seven. Resumed building. Mortar from week five had cured well under the covers. No frost damage visible.
3.3: By Wednesday, reached full height at 54 inches. Began laying the coping stones — the original numbered ones, with three new ones cut to match the cracked originals.
3.4: Thursday. Coping complete. Pointed all joints on the visible face with a neat flush finish. Brushed excess mortar before it set.
3.5: Friday. Final inspection. Wall plumb, level, and solid. Cleaned up the site. Total project: seven weeks, original estimate was four. The drainage work and frost added three weeks, but the wall will outlast us both.
